text:
Austral Ecology




   RESEARCH ARTICLE



Reversal of Rainforest Invasion and Recovery After
Drought in Australian Savanna
R. J. Fensham1,2 | J. Drimer2 | H. Macdermott1

1Department of Environment, Tourism, Science and Innovation, Queensland Herbarium and Biodiversity Science, Toowong, Queensland,

Australia | 2School of the Environment, University of Queensland, St Lucia, Queensland, Australia

Correspondence: R. J. Fensham (r.fensham@uq.edu.au)

Received: 1 April 2025 | Revised: 11 September 2025 | Accepted: 17 September 2025

Keywords: Australia | drought | eucalypt | fire | rainforest invasion | savanna


  ABSTRACT
  Fire and drought are important influences on the structure and floristics of tropical savanna, but determining the sensitivity of
  rainforest invasion in savanna to fire and recovery after drought requires long-­term monitoring. A savanna in north Queensland
  invaded by rainforest after a long fire-­free period was monitored within a 1.56 ha plot after drought and subject to nine fires over
  16 years. Savanna tree densities increased, mostly resulting from suckering, but rainforest trees declined to 19% of the pre-­burn

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Austral Ecology - 2025 - Fensham - Reversal of Rainforest Invasion and Recovery After Drought in Australian Savanna.pdf
text:
t after drought and subject to nine fires over
  16 years. Savanna tree densities increased, mostly resulting from suckering, but rainforest trees declined to 19% of the pre-­burn
  density. Savanna basal area declined halfway through the monitoring period to 9.6% of the original measurement after drought.
  By contrast, rainforest basal area was reduced to 0.0 m2 ha−1 in the same period after being subject to four fires and remained at
  zero for the course of monitoring. Savanna basal area recovered to 16.9% of its original value by the end of monitoring. The study
  confirms the fire resilience of Australian savanna trees, Eucalyptus and Corymbia, relative to rainforest trees and indicates that
  regular burning maintains savanna in a pure state without rainforest invasion. Nevertheless, drought caused the collapse of the
  savanna tree layer despite an absence of livestock grazing. Such declines and gradual recovery are a natural dynamic in savanna
  with multi-­year rainfall deficit.

decline in the density of all rainforest species (Figure 2a).          Data Availability Statement

Supporting Information
Additional supporting information can be found online in the Supporting
Information section. Appendix S1: Fire history at Pyro Pocket, Dry
fuel weight was measured typically with 12, 25 × 25 cm frames. Rate of
spread was recorded in at least five locations and averaged. Appendix

source: /run/media/jpereiratrindade/labeco10T/labeco02/Revisao/Austral Ecology - 2025 - Fensham - Reversal of Rainforest Invasion and Recovery After Drought in Australian Savanna.pdf
text:
x S1: Fire history at Pyro Pocket, Dry
fuel weight was measured typically with 12, 25 × 25 cm frames. Rate of
spread was recorded in at least five locations and averaged. Appendix
S2: Numbers of trees (< 20 cm dbh) recorded during the monitoring in-
tervals on the 1.56 ha plot. Eucalyptus xanthoclada and Corymbia eryth-
rophloia are the only species with individual > 20 cm dbh (in brackets).
Recruits (R) are those plants that were not present at the previous moni-
toring time and are mostly a result of vegetative reproduction. Survivors
(S) are those individuals that were present at the previous monitoring
time. Nomenclature follows Bean (2023).
